Barbicane established the fact, by reiterated observation, that the reliefs on the borders of the disc, although they had been acted upon by different forces to those of the central region, presented a uniform conformation. There was the same circular aggregation, the same accidents of ground. Still it might be supposed that their arrangements were not completely analogous. In the centre the still malleable crust of the moon suffered the double attraction of the moon and the earth acting in inverse ways according to a radius prolonged from one to the other. On the borders of the disc, on the contrary, the lunar attraction has been, thus to say, perpendicular with the terrestrial attraction. It seems, therefore, that the reliefs on the soil produced under these conditions ought to have taken a different form. Yet they had not, therefore the moon had found in herself alone the principle of her formation and constitution. She owed nothing to foreign influences, which justified the remarkable proposition of Arago's, "No action exterior to the moon has contributed to the production of her relief."

[MI-023-001]It was now exactly seven months since the balloon voyagers had been thrown on [[lincoln-island|Lincoln Island]]. No vestiges of his handiwork showed that either at an early or at a late period had man lived there. Not only did it now appear to be uninhabited by any but themselves, but the colonists were compelled to believe that it never had been inhabited. And now, all this scaffolding of reasonings fell before a simple ball of metal, found in the body of an inoffensive rodent! description: The transcontinental North American republic, home of the Gun Club and a recurring stage for Vernean voyages.

The Aral Sea is the great inland sea of Central Asia, lying between the Ural steppe and the plateau of the [[Pamir]]. It is drying up, as the Caspian will dry up, an evaporation explained by the powerful concentration of the solar rays on the territories between the Sea of Aral and the plateau of the Pamir. (ASC-006-027)

Chicago is the booming American railway city that marks a crucial stage of the transcontinental crossing in *[[Around the World in Eighty Days]]*. When Phileas Fogg's party reaches it, the city is "already risen from its ruins, and more proudly seated than ever on the borders of its beautiful Lake Michigan" (AWED-031-036), rebuilt after the great fire.

Tripoli is a city and stretch of North African coast, the northern gateway to the Great Desert. In the Vernean corpus it is above all the departure and return point of the Saharan explorers whose routes Dr. Ferguson's balloon retraces in *Five Weeks in a Balloon*.
